description: Full styling design pass per DEC-024. Originally deferred four times (DEC-024 → DEC-026 → DEC-037 → DEC-042) on the CBM-redo-friction trigger principle. **Reopened as a parallel workstream by DEC-076 (05-16-26)** because the trigger condition (CBM-redo Phase 1 surfacing visual friction on the four v0.4 methodology panels) cannot fire in v0.5's timeframe — CBM redo waits on v0.5 (DEC-075) plus the paper-test (DEC-077) plus Phase 1 itself, and the four-deferral pattern with progressively-elaborate trigger conditions that never fire has become an anti-pattern. The pass establishes a coherent visual language for the v2 desktop application: design tokens (palette with semantic naming, typography scale, spacing scale, radius/border/elevation tokens, density target), visual decisions for major component classes (panels, sidebar, buttons, form controls, dialogs, tables), and implementation across all governance and methodology panels. **Workstream structure (per `PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/styling-workstream-plan.md`):** two conversations — Conversation 1 produces the design pass document at `PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/styling-design-pass.md`; Conversation 2 produces the styling PRD, implementation plan, and slice build prompts. Strawman five build slices follow (foundation; governance panel retrofit; methodology panel retrofit; dialog and form-control polish; closeout). **Boundary discipline with v0.5 (per DEC-076):** styling owns the visual layer (QSS, design tokens, panel chrome, sidebar visuals, About dialog, hover/focus/disabled states); v0.5 owns the data/routing layer (config, ActiveEngagementContext, alembic, API routing, engagement entity). One coupling point: v0.5's engagement panel inherits whatever tokens are current at slice land time; either order ships fine. **Version-bundling question** (v0.5 vs. v0.6) deferred to the styling workstream's Conversation 2.
title: Full styling design pass per DEC-024

description:
**Explain it like I was 5:** When you want to add a new note to the project's notebook, you have to first peek at the last page to figure out what number to write at the top of yours — and if you forget, the notebook yells at you and won't take it. PI-002 makes the notebook smart enough to pick the next page number itself: you just hand it the note. You can still pick your own page number if you want to.

DEC-043 resolved the SES-010 identifier-asymmetry friction via option (B) — ``GET /<entity>/next-identifier`` helpers for all twelve prefixed entity types. Option (C) — making ``identifier`` optional in POST bodies with server-side auto-assignment on omission — is the cleaner end state but was deferred from v0.4 because it changes existing endpoint contracts (gray-area under the kickoff's 'doesn't alter v0.3's storage shape' rule) and deserves its own design pass. Open design questions: (1) how to disambiguate client-omitted identifier (auto-assign) from client-specified-empty-string identifier (validation error)? (2) does this apply to versioned entity types like charter and status, whose identifiers are version numbers? (3) does the MCP server need any change beyond what the API change implies? Target release: v0.5 or later, depending on scope competition.
title: Make `identifier` optional in POST bodies (SES-010 option C)

description: DEC-039 deferred persona from v0.4's minimum-viable inventory because evolved Phase 1's interview guide v0.2 explicitly excludes persona elicitation in Phase 1 — persona context comes from pre-engagement reading of operational role definitions, used as consultant background rather than captured as records. Phase 2 or 3 of the evolved methodology may surface persona records (actors performing processes, role-based access boundaries). The persona entity type design should follow the workstream pattern (one Claude.ai conversation produces the schema spec, design-only, separate from build). Target release: v0.5+, conditional on CBM redo Phase 2 or 3 surfacing need.
title: Persona entity type for v0.5+ (deferred from v0.4 minimum-viable inventory)

description: DEC-039 deferred fields, requirements, manual-config items, and test specs from v0.4's minimum-viable inventory. These are Phase 3+ entity types in the evolved methodology (Iteration Build and Deploy through Engagement Closure and Adoption). Each will need its own schema-design conversation following the workstream pattern established in v0.4. Field is the most urgent of the four because entity (shipped thin in v0.4) gains real utility only when fields can attach. Likely v0.5 ships field; later releases ship the rest. Target release: v0.5+ for field; v0.6+ for the rest, conditional on CBM redo progression and v2 patterns established in earlier releases.
title: Additional methodology entity types for v0.5+: field, requirement, manual_config, test_spec

description: DEC-039 ships process in v0.4 with a thin Phase-1-only schema: identifier, name, brief description, priority classification, domain reference, process-to-process connections. Phase 3 of the evolved methodology (Iteration Build and Deploy) produces full Process Documents with steps, actors (personas), entity-field touches, triggers, outcomes, edge cases. Growing process's schema to host this content is v0.5+ work that depends on field (PI-004) and persona (PI-003) existing as records. Likely a migration story rather than re-design (thin schema designed with growth in mind), but the migration scope is non-trivial. Target release: v0.5+ after persona and field land, conditional on CBM redo reaching Phase 3.
title: Process schema growth beyond Phase 1 thin shape (full process definition)

description: DEC-046 establishes the parent-prefix field-naming convention for methodology entities forward-only; governance entities (DEC, SES, RSK, PI, TOP, REF, CHR, STA) retain bare field names (status, title, name, description, etc.) until this retrofit lands. Retrofit scope: Alembic migrations renaming columns on eight existing tables; access-layer method updates across crmbuilder-v2/src/crmbuilder_v2/access/; REST API serialization updates; MCP tool input/output updates; UI dialog updates (every QLineEdit, QComboBox, and table model column bound to a renamed field); DB-export JSON snapshot regeneration with rewriting of committed snapshots in git history; backward-compat considerations for any external scripts hitting the REST API or MCP. Substantial migration. The v0.4-build-planning conversation decides whether to pull this retrofit into v0.4 scope (significant additional work) or defer to v0.5+. The methodology workstream's specs ship with the new convention regardless of when this retrofit lands.
title: Retrofit governance entities to parent-prefix field-naming convention

description: The existing CBM methodology uses 2-letter domain codes (MN for Mentoring, MR for Mentor Recruitment, CR for Client Recruiting, FU for Fundraising) as prefixes for process and entity identifiers (MN-INTAKE, MR-RECRUIT, etc.). The Phase 1 evolved-methodology interview guide section 7.2 says the Domain Inventory includes 'name in client's language and a structural code' but does not specify whether downstream process/entity codes continue to use mnemonic prefixes in the evolved methodology. DEC-045 defers domain_short_code from the v0.4 minimum-viable shape pending CBM-redo signal. If real-engagement Phase 3 work confirms that mnemonic prefixes still pull weight for human-readable process/entity identifier construction, a v0.5 schema migration adds domain_short_code (str, 2-4 uppercase letters, unique within engagement); the process and entity schemas adopt it for human-readable identifier prefixes alongside their numeric identifiers. If the evolved methodology converges on sequential identifiers without mnemonic prefixes, this PI closes as not-needed.
title: domain.short_code field for mnemonic references and downstream identifier prefixes

description: Surfaced by SES-012 close-out friction. The current pattern requires Doug to either (a) copy-paste session content into the v0.3 desktop New Session dialog and run a per-conversation apply script for decisions/PIs/references, or (b) run a generic apply_close_out.py script that processes a JSON payload file (mid-SES-012 mitigation). Both still require a manual command after each schema-design conversation. The architectural fix: build an inbox folder watcher into the v0.3 desktop application. The app monitors a known directory (e.g., crmbuilder-v2/inbox/ or PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/close-out-payloads/), detects new JSON payload files, prompts the user to review (showing a summary of what would be written), and on confirmation imports all records via the existing access layer. Processed files move to inbox/processed/ for traceability. Idempotent on re-run (HTTP 409 = already present, skip). Implementation scope: file-watch infrastructure (already exists for db-export refresh — possibly reusable), payload-schema validation, summary-and-confirm modal, error reporting per record. The v0.4-build-planning conversation decides whether to pull this into v0.4 scope (eliminates close-out friction for all four workstream conversations) or defer to v0.5+. If deferred, the apply_close_out.py script remains the close-out pattern.
title: Inbox folder watcher in v0.3 desktop app for close-out JSON payloads

description: DEC-054 defers a Domains column on the master pane of the Entities panel to v0.5+, jointly enabled by PI-007 (domain.short_code field). The column renders affiliated domains as comma-separated short codes (e.g., 'MN, MR, FU') once short codes exist; without them, DOM-NNN identifiers don't tell a consultant anything at a glance and the column's UX value is lost. Implementation scope when both PI-007 and PI-009 land together: (a) access-layer batched-join query (list_entities_with_affiliations() or equivalent) returning each entity's affiliated-domain short codes alongside its scalar fields without N+1 queries; (b) ListDetailPanel view-model update to surface the new column; (c) column-width and sorting behavior decisions (sort by what? probably alphabetical-by-short-code-list with secondary sort by entity_name). Prioritization signal: CBM redo will reveal whether the absence of the column in v0.4 is high-friction (do consultants frequently click into the detail pane to check affiliations? do they request a list view grouped by domain?). If high-friction, PI-009 + PI-007 move up in v0.5 priority; if low-friction, they stay queued.
title: Master-pane Domains column on the Entities panel (paired with PI-007 short codes)

description: DEC-051 and DEC-053 jointly defer two entity-schema growth areas to v0.5+: (a) entity variants — the Mentor Contact / Client Contact pattern from CBM history where two related-but-distinct entities share a base type but carry different fields and role semantics; (b) entity base-type / kind classification — person / organization / event / transaction-style categorization that informs CRM-engine selection and field-shape defaults. Both are intentionally absent from v0.4: variants because Phase 1 doesn't produce them and v0.4 has no fields to vary across them; base-type/kind because it's Phase 3 territory tied to CRM-engine specifics that v0.4's thin shape stays neutral about. Tracked separately from PI-004 (which groups field/requirement/manual_config/test_spec as new methodology entity types) because PI-010 is entity-schema growth — extending the existing entity type — not introducing new entity types. Variant mechanism TBD: candidates include self-referential FK (entity_parent_identifier), references-entity edge (entity_variant_of_entity), or a separate variant entity type sitting between base entity and field. Mechanism choice is a v0.5+ design conversation. Workaround in v0.4: variants are distinguished by suffixing entity_name ('Contact — Mentor', 'Contact — Client'); CBM redo will reveal how much friction the workaround creates and inform mechanism design. Base-type/kind classification: candidate v0.5+ shape is an enum field (entity_kind: person | organization | event | transaction | other) on the entity table, with validation that informs Phase 3 field-shape defaults and Phase 5 CRM-evaluation scoring.
title: Entity-schema v0.5+ extensions — variants and base-type/kind classification

description: DEC-057 adopted process_classification (mission_critical / supporting / deferred / unclassified) as the methodology priority classification per Principle 3. During the conversation the user observed that this is a classification, not a conventional scalar priority, and the methodology's three buckets don't answer the implementation-ranking question — 'of the mission-critical processes, which do we build first?'. A separate scalar implementation-priority field (working name process_priority, values Critical / High / Medium / Low or 1-5 or similar) is deferred to v0.5+. The deferral reasoning: Phase 1 does not formally produce per-process implementation priority — that information emerges from Phase 2 Slice Planning, which v0.4 schema does not yet host as content. When Phase 2 (Slice Planning) iteration content lands in v0.5+, the scalar priority field is part of that work, scoped per-iteration rather than global to the process record (the same process might have different scalar priorities in different iterations). Implementation when this PI lands: (a) add process_priority field with chosen value space; (b) decide whether the field is global on the process record or per-iteration via a join table to a future Iteration entity; (c) UI: master-pane column showing classification + priority paired (e.g., 'mission_critical / High'); (d) priority-transition validation if any (probably free movement per Principle 7). Prioritization signal: CBM redo Phase 1 will reveal whether consultants want implementation-priority ranking at Phase 1 cadence (probably no — Phase 1 outputs classification, not implementation order). Real signal comes from Phase 2 when slice planning content needs to host iteration-membership decisions.
title: Future scalar implementation-priority field on process for ranking within classification buckets

description:
DEC-061 adopted Option A (thinnest) for the crm_candidate field inventory under minimum-viable v0.4 scope. Four optional metadata fields named in the schema-design-kickoff-crm_candidate.md document were deferred from v0.4 to v0.5+ under this planning item:

- crm_candidate_vendor_url — optional free-text URL field pointing to the vendor's product or documentation page; no enum constraint, light URL-shape validation only.
- crm_candidate_hosting_type — optional enum: 'cloud' | 'self_hosted' | 'both'. Captures the Phase 1 hosting selection criterion as a structured value.
- crm_candidate_license_type — optional enum: 'open_source' | 'commercial' | 'freemium'. Captures the Phase 1 license/cost-model selection criterion as a structured value.
- crm_candidate_price_tier — optional enum: 'free' | 'paid'. Coarse capture of the Phase 1 budget selection criterion.

The deferral rationale is the minimum-viable v0.4 scope principle plus the small per-engagement candidate count (2-3 records typically), which makes scannability gains from structured fields small relative to their schema-and-UI-and-migration cost. The Phase 1 selection criteria all live in crm_candidate_fit_reason prose in v0.4.

Triggers to pull this PI to v0.5 ahead of other v0.5 candidates:
- CBM-redo reveals that consultants miss scannable classification at the master-pane level when reviewing the candidate set across iterations (the CBM-redo-friction trigger pattern established by DEC-042 for PI-001).
- Phase 3 deployment-log entity type or Phase 4 Comparison Artifact entity type (both deferred to v0.5+ entity types under PI-004 expansion) need to relate to structured candidate metadata for cross-iteration analysis.
- Multi-engagement analytics emerge as a v2 use case where parsing crm_candidate_fit_reason prose for hosting / license / budget across engagements is infeasible.

Implementation scope when pulled:
- Alembic migration extending the crm_candidates table with four optional new columns: vendor_url TEXT (nullable, no CHECK constraint), hosting_type TEXT (nullable, CHECK constraint on the three-value enum), license_type TEXT (nullable, CHECK constraint on the three-value enum), price_tier TEXT (nullable, CHECK constraint on the two-value enum). All new columns are nullable; existing crm_candidate records persist with the new columns NULL.
- Access-layer validators for the three enum fields (the URL field gets light client-side and access-layer URL-shape validation; the enums match the standard pattern used by domain_status, entity_status, process_classification).
- vocab.py expansion: not needed; the enums are non-reference values stored directly in the entity table.
- UI work: combo boxes for the three enums in the create/edit dialog and detail pane; free-text URL editor for vendor_url; possibly a master-pane column extension to render one or more of the new classification fields as scannable badges. Specific UI shape decided at v0.5+ planning time.
- Acceptance criteria additions for the four new fields: format validation, NULL-on-existing-records, transition validation if any enum values are constrained to transition rules (working assumption: no — classification fields here describe stable product attributes, not lifecycle states).

No backward-incompatible changes. The crm_candidate API surface stays at the standard 8 endpoints; the new fields are accepted as optional in POST / PATCH / PUT bodies and surfaced in GET responses.
title: crm_candidate structured-metadata enums (vendor_url, hosting_type, license_type, price_tier) for v0.5+

description:
Target version: v0.5+.

The original methodology named Cross-Domain Service (Notes, Email, Calendar, Surveys) as a methodology entity type consumed across domains. The evolved methodology has not yet resolved whether Cross-Domain Service remains a distinct entity type, is subsumed into `process` once `process_kind` lands in v0.5+, or is dropped. The v0.4 schema workstream did not address the question and the original methodology-entity-schema-planning-prompt did not flag the gap. Resolution waits on CBM-redo signal: if Phase 1 surfaces work that the evolved methodology treats as cross-domain in nature, evaluate whether `process` with a future `process_kind` field is the right home, or whether a separate `cross_domain_service` entity type is warranted. Decision gate: at the first CBM-redo Phase 1 conversation that touches Notes / Email / Calendar / Surveys content. Surfaced by the 05-14-26 reconciliation conversation (SES-018).
title: Cross-Domain Service representation

description:
Target version: v0.5+.

The catalog ingestion PRD's section 3.3 sketched a hybrid integration pattern: methodology entities carry an optional `primary_catalog_entity_id` FK plus DEC-006 universal references for weak ties. The four v0.4 methodology schemas (`domain`, `entity`, `process`, `crm_candidate`) do not implement the FK because their MVS thin shape doesn't carry the field-level entity definitions that would need it. When PI-004's `field` entity type lands in v0.5+, the catalog FK question must be settled: (a) integer FK to `catalog_entity.id` vs text FK to `catalog_entity.catalog_id` per the catalog DEC-065 stable-identifier convention; (b) FK on `entity` vs on `field`; (c) how universal-references weak ties interact with the FK semantics. Scope: tied to PI-004 prerequisite; design conversation opens when PI-004's design conversation does, not independently. Surfaced by the 05-14-26 reconciliation conversation (SES-018).
title: Catalog FK integration for methodology entities

description:
Target version: v0.5+ or later.

DEC-008 prescribes that v2 produces renders, not authored copies — Word documents, deployment YAML, and JSON exports are all generated from the database, not maintained alongside it. v0.4 ships the storage and UI surface for the four MVS methodology entity types but zero renderer work. Three render targets are forecast: (a) .docx generation for Domain Inventory and Phase 1 Prioritized Backbone documents (per evolved-methodology Phase 1 outputs); (b) YAML generation for the v1 deployment engine (current YAML lives in client repositories as hand-authored files); (c) per-entity JSON exports for git-diff via the existing DEC-008 hook (these may already exist mechanically through the storage layer's standard export pattern — verify before scoping). Likely a sizable workstream on its own; opening it before v0.4 ships is premature. A separate planning conversation should follow either v0.4 closeout or first CBM-redo iteration, whichever comes first. Surfaced by the 05-14-26 reconciliation conversation (SES-018).
title: Methodology entity renderers

description:
Target version: v0.5+.

The live `/references` router does not enforce per-pair `RELATIONSHIP_RULES` because doing so would break v0.3 regression tests that use cross-pair references like `session → decision / decided_in`. Per-pair vocab compliance is currently enforced only at the cascading `ReferenceCreateDialog`, which means the desktop UI is safe but the REST surface is not. If the API is consumed only by the desktop UI (which routes through the dialog), the gap is theoretical. If MCP tools or any external script POSTs directly to `/references` with arbitrary kinds, a non-vocab `kind` could land in the database. Resolution: tighten router-level validation while preserving v0.3 cross-pair governance reference behavior — likely by enforcing per-pair `RELATIONSHIP_RULES` only for pairs where rules exist, leaving pairs with no rules (current v0.3 pattern) unconstrained. Surfaced by SES-021 (v0.4 slice C entities panel build) when implementing criterion 14's "POST /references with (entity, domain) and an unsupported kind returns 422" test, which was satisfied at the schema-rejection level rather than at the router-vocab level.
title: Router-level per-pair vocabulary enforcement on `/references`

description:
Anchored by DEC-081. v0.5 ships one API and MCP subprocess per engagement (Option A); multi-tenant single-process server (Option B) is the target architecture for v2's production phase. The migration lands when v2 transitions from prototype (v0.x releases) to production (v1.0). Trigger is a planned transition, not a friction signal (avoiding the PI-001 anti-pattern of friction triggers that never fire).

Scope of the migration when it lands:

1. Refactor every REST endpoint to accept engagement context. Two viable mechanisms: a request header (X-Engagement: ENG-NNN) or a path-prefix (/v1/engagements/ENG-NNN/sessions, etc.). The mechanism is settled when the workstream opens; both options have precedent in similar systems.

2. Refactor the access-layer connection model to hold per-engagement connections. Options include: a connection pool keyed by engagement_identifier opened lazily on first use; eager pool warming at startup; or per-request connection acquire/release with engagement-context binding. The pool strategy is settled by the workstream.

3. Refactor tests for engagement-context fixtures. Every test that today asserts behavior against a single DB needs to express which engagement it operates against. v0.4's test suite is the regression net.

4. Reshape the MCP server to mirror the multi-tenant API. MCP tools either gain an engagement-context input argument or a per-tool engagement selector. Specific shape is settled by the workstream.

5. Remove the kill-relaunch dance from the desktop's engagement-switch path. The desktop's ActiveEngagementContext continues to exist but switching becomes a fast in-process operation rather than a process-lifecycle dance. The 'Switching engagement...' UX indicator becomes vestigial or is removed.

6. Consider adding an activate API endpoint (POST /engagements/{identifier}/activate) at this time. The endpoint becomes possible once the API server can switch engagements without restarting; whether to expose it depends on the workstream's posture on cross-engagement scripting use cases.

Dependencies and ordering: PI-017's workstream is sequenced after v0.5 ships and after enough real-use signal has accumulated (CBM Phase 1 underway or completed; possibly additional client engagements in flight) to validate that the prototype-to-production transition is the right moment. The exact timing is determined at the production-readiness workstream's opening kickoff conversation, not pre-committed here.

Note: this PI is not the same as the deferred 'cross-engagement reporting' or 'engagement-level access control' workstreams. Those are separate v0.6+ candidates. PI-017 is strictly the infrastructure migration; cross-engagement features become technically feasible after PI-017 ships but are out of PI-017's scope.
title: Migrate API and MCP servers to multi-tenant model

description:
**Context.** The CRM Builder YAML schema currently exposes only `oneToMany`, `manyToOne`, and `manyToMany` link types. `oneToOne` is rejected by the validator (`VALID_LINK_TYPES` in `espo_impl/core/config_loader.py:72`). EspoCRM's underlying `EntityManager/action/createLink` API supports oneToOne natively, so this is a translation-layer gap, not a target-system limitation. Two methodology-side artifacts already assume oneToOne is valid (`PRDs/process/interviews/interview-entity-prd.md:338` lists it; `PRDs/_archive/automation-prompts/CLAUDE-CODE-PROMPT-step16-importer-cleanup-3.md:70` accepts it in the Path B importer), so requirements written through the methodology can capture `oneToOne` and then become un-deployable when translated to YAML. The first concrete trigger surfaced 2026-05-19 in the CBM MN-Partner-TEST.yaml / MN-Sponsor-TEST.yaml scaffolding (Account 1:1 PartnerProfile, Account 1:1 SponsorProfile) where the workaround is `manyToOne` + an operator-discipline / duplicate-check uniqueness rule.

**Goal.** A YAML author can write `linkType: oneToOne` in a `relationships:` block; the validator accepts it; the engine creates a true 1:1 link in EspoCRM with the correct asymmetric internal types (`hasOne` on one side, `belongsTo` on the inverse).

**Files to touch.**
1. `espo_impl/core/config_loader.py:72` — add `"oneToOne"` to `VALID_LINK_TYPES`.
2. `espo_impl/core/relationship_manager.py` (~line 25, `LINK_TYPE_TO_METADATA`) — current mapping is single-row-per-linkType (`oneToMany→hasMany`, `manyToOne→belongsTo`, `manyToMany→hasMany`). oneToOne is asymmetric: one side is `hasOne`, the other is `belongsTo`. Restructure to a per-direction mapping, e.g. `(linkType, side) → metadataType`, or emit oneToOne-aware payload inside `_build_payload`.
3. `espo_impl/core/relationship_manager.py:260` (`_links_match`) — the comparator reads `existing.get("type")` and compares against the expected type from the mapping. With oneToOne it needs to know which side it is reading and which expected type to compare. Probably refactor to compare both sides via two GETs.
4. `espo_impl/core/relationship_manager.py:280` (`_build_payload`) — `linkType` field in the payload is the YAML value verbatim; confirm EspoCRM's `createLink` endpoint accepts the string `"oneToOne"` as-is (it does in current EspoCRM versions). Otherwise emit `hasOne` / `belongsTo` per side.
5. Validator additions in `espo_impl/core/config_loader.py` `_validate_relationship` (~line 2143): oneToOne with `relationName` set should warn (relationName is a manyToMany affordance); oneToOne missing `linkForeign` should be a hard error.

**Steps.**
1. Read the EspoCRM source for `EntityManager.actionCreateLink` to confirm the oneToOne payload shape it expects.
2. Add `oneToOne` to `VALID_LINK_TYPES`; add the asymmetric mapping; refactor `_build_payload` if needed.
3. Refactor `_links_match` to compare oneToOne links correctly (the type read back will differ per side).
4. Add unit tests covering: valid oneToOne accepted; oneToOne without linkForeign rejected; oneToOne with relationName warns.
5. Add an end-to-end test that creates a oneToOne against a real EspoCRM (or a recorded fixture) and verifies the link metadata round-trips.
6. Update `PRDs/product/app-yaml-schema.md` Section 6 (relationships) to document oneToOne.
7. Update `MN-Partner-TEST.yaml` and `MN-Sponsor-TEST.yaml` in the CBM repo to use `linkType: oneToOne` once the engine supports it; drop the manyToOne + manual-uniqueness workaround.

**Validation.**
- `uv run pytest tests/ -k relationship` passes.
- Deploy the updated MN-Partner-TEST.yaml against a fresh EspoCRM and confirm: (a) Account.partnerProfile is a single-record link (not a list panel); (b) attempting to link a second PartnerProfile to the same Account is rejected by EspoCRM at the schema level; (c) deleting the linked PartnerProfile clears the link on Account.

**References.**
- Conversation 2026-05-19 (CBM MN partner/sponsor YAML scaffolding).
- Original relationship-manager commit: `f0bf763` (scope explicitly listed three types, no rationale for omitting oneToOne).
- Related engine-bug entries in CLAUDE.md: cross-batch field reference resolution, topological deploy ordering — independent of this PI but in the same engine-evolution backlog.
title: Add oneToOne relationship support to YAML schema

description:
**Context.** The YAML validator's layout-category check is file-scoped. `_validate_layout` in `espo_impl/core/config_loader.py:663-665` builds `field_categories` only from the same YAML's `entity.fields` block:

```python
field_categories = {
    f.category for f in entity.fields if f.category
}
```

This means a layout panel referencing `category: "Sponsor Profile"` only validates if the same YAML file declares a field with that category — it ignores fields declared in sibling YAMLs in the deploy batch and fields already on the server.

This is asymmetric with the same function's handling of field references in `visibleWhen` conditions (line 658-662), which DOES union across `ProgramContext.field_names_for(entity.name)` and `_native_field_names(entity)`. The category check is a one-line addition away from being consistent with the field-name check.

**Triggers seen.** 2026-05-19 conversation on CBM MN-Account / MN-Partner / MN-Sponsor file split. The user wanted to extract Partner-conditional and Sponsor-conditional fields into separate YAMLs while keeping the layout panels in MN-Account.yaml. Failed because the validator wouldn't accept layout panels whose categories were declared in a sibling file. Workaround chosen: keep field+layout in same file or use Account-side reference fields with categories.

**Goal.** A YAML file can declare a layout panel with a category whose backing field is in (a) the same file, (b) a sibling YAML in the same deploy batch, or (c) already on the server. (a) is the current behavior; (b) is a `ProgramContext` consultation; (c) is a server-state consultation when an instance is connected.

**Files to touch.**
1. `espo_impl/core/config_loader.py:663-665` (`_validate_layout`, `field_categories` construction) — union with:
   - Categories from `self._active_context.field_categories_for(entity.name)` (new method on `ProgramContext`, parallels existing `field_names_for`).
   - Categories from server state if an instance is connected (new optional parameter or attribute carrying the instance's metadata).
2. `espo_impl/core/models.py` `ProgramContext` — add `field_categories_by_entity: dict[str, set[str]]` populated from the union of all programs' fields' categories. Parallel to existing `fields_by_entity`.
3. `espo_impl/core/config_loader.py:310` `validate_program_with_context` — already accepts a `ProgramContext`; ensure the new categories field is consumed.
4. (Optional, server-state union) — wire an `EspoAdminClient` reference into the loader so `_native_field_names` and the new categories method can call `GET /Metadata?key=entityDefs.{Entity}.fields` and extract `category` per field. Falls back gracefully to batch-only when no client is available.

**Steps.**
1. Add `field_categories_by_entity` to `ProgramContext`; populate it in the `from_programs` factory.
2. Update `_validate_layout` to union `entity.fields`-derived categories with the context's `field_categories_for(entity.name)`.

description:
**Context.** EspoCRM stores ONE detail layout per entity. The layout-save endpoint (`PUT {entity}/layout/{layoutType}` in `espo_impl/core/api_client.py:484`) replaces the entire layout payload on each call. The deploy engine processes YAML files one at a time and writes layouts from each file independently. If two or more YAML files target the same entity and both declare a `layout:` block, only the last to deploy survives — the prior file's panels are clobbered.

This breaks the methodology pattern where a domain (e.g., MN, FU, CR) wants to contribute its own panels to a shared native entity (Account, Contact) without one file owning the entire layout. The current workaround is: one file owns the full layout for an entity, others limit themselves to fields and let the layout-owning file reference categories declared elsewhere (which itself requires PI-019). For new custom entities introduced in a single file, the single-owner pattern is fine; for native entities extended across multiple domains, it's awkward.

**Triggers seen.** 2026-05-19 CBM conversation on splitting Sponsor/Partner fields out of MN-Account.yaml. With current engine: MN-Sponsor-TEST.yaml cannot contribute a Sponsor Profile panel to the Account layout because MN-Account.yaml would clobber it (or vice versa). Workaround: keep all Account-layout panels in MN-Account.yaml.

**Goal.** A YAML author can declare a `layout:` block in any YAML that targets an entity, and the engine merges all panels from all batched YAMLs targeting that entity into a single deployed layout. Panel ordering is deterministic (by file alphabetical order, then by declaration order within a file). Conflicts (e.g., two files declaring a panel with the same `label`) are flagged as deploy errors.

**Files to touch.**
1. `espo_impl/core/layout_manager.py:56` (`LayoutManager.process_layouts`) — currently called once per YAML file with that file's `entity.layout` block. Refactor entry point so it accepts the full batch (or its layout contributions per entity) and emits one consolidated `save_layout` call per (entity, layoutType).
2. `espo_impl/workers/run_worker.py` — change the orchestration so layout writes run AFTER all per-file field/relationship/entity work completes (currently layouts run per-file in the field/layout/relationship phase). New phase: "AggregateLayouts" between the per-file work and the workflows phase.
3. `espo_impl/core/layout_manager.py:392` (`_auto_generate_rows`) — currently filters `field_definitions` by category against THIS file's fields. After aggregation, should consider all fields across all batched files targeting the entity. Depends on PI-019 (cross-file category resolution) being in place at validation time so the panels can validate.
4. New conflict-detection logic: two files contributing a panel with the same `label` for the same entity should produce a deploy error like `Layout conflict: Account.detail panel 'Client Profile' declared by MN-Account.yaml and CR-Account.yaml`.
5. Panel-order policy: document and implement deterministic ordering. Option A: file alphabetical order then declaration order. Option B: each file declares a `panelOrder` integer per panel. Option A is simpler; B is more flexible.

**Steps.**
1. Settle the panel-order policy (A or B). Add to the YAML schema doc.
2. Refactor `RunWorker` orchestration to defer layout writes until after the per-file work.
3. Add a `LayoutAggregator` (or extend `LayoutManager`) that collects layout contributions across the batch and emits one save per entity+layoutType.
4. Add the conflict-detection pass.
5. Update `_auto_generate_rows` to consult cross-file field definitions (depends on PI-019).
6. Update unit tests; add end-to-end tests that exercise the multi-file Account layout case.
7. Update `PRDs/product/app-yaml-schema.md` to document the aggregation semantics and panel-ordering rule.
8. Once landed, split CBM's MN-Account / MN-Partner-TEST / MN-Sponsor-TEST so each file contributes its own panel block and the engine merges them — drop the SPLIT-NOTE caveats in those files.

**Validation.**
- `uv run pytest tests/ -k layout` passes (existing single-file tests must still pass).
- A new test deploys two YAMLs (MN-Account.yaml with Client Profile panel only, MN-Sponsor-TEST.yaml with Sponsor Profile panel) and verifies the resulting EspoCRM Account detail layout has BOTH panels in deterministic order.
- A conflict test: two YAMLs declaring panels with the same label on the same entity produces a deploy error.

**References.**
- Conversation 2026-05-19 (CBM partner/sponsor YAML file-split exploration; the SPLIT-NOTE blocks in MN-Account.yaml / MN-Partner-TEST.yaml / MN-Sponsor-TEST.yaml document this gap).
- Depends on PI-019 for the validator side (categories declared in sibling files must be resolvable at validation time).
- Related but distinct: the engine-bug-backlog entry about topological deploy ordering (relationship dependencies) — separate concern, doesn't block this.
title: Cross-file layout aggregation in deploy engine

description:
Build and integrate `crmbuilder/tools/workstream_reconcile.py` to prevent state-drift between git-committed workstream deliverables and the V2 governance database at session-startup time.

**Why this exists.** SES-050 (the reference_book entity schema-design conversation) opened against `PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/schema-design-kickoff-reference-book.md` as a fresh schema-design conversation, but the first pre-flight check found that the deliverable (`governance-schema-specs/reference_book.md`, 467 lines at v1.0) had already been drafted and committed at `c1d007e` on 05-21-26 14:55 by a prior Claude.ai sandbox session whose close-out was never run. The V2 database had no SES-050 or DEC-135..140 yet recorded; git was one commit ahead of origin/main with the spec on it, but the governance database was silently out of sync. Without the manual `git log` check during SES-050's pre-flight, the schema-design conversation would have either overwritten 467 lines of completed work or duplicated effort to produce a parallel spec. The root cause is a two-system-of-record gap: git holds committed deliverables; the V2 database holds governance state; the bridge — manual close-out apply — is a separate step that can be skipped silently.

**Discharge conditions** (all must be met to mark PI-023 Resolved):

1. `crmbuilder/tools/workstream_reconcile.py` exists, runs from any working directory, and accepts a workstream identifier as input. (Once the V2 workstream entity ships per the governance-schema workstream's build phase, the tool may accept a workstream record directly; until then, the workstream identifier is supplied as a string with the deliverable directory implied by convention.)

2. The utility queries the V2 API for the workstream's conversations (via the sessions table for pre-workstream-entity workstreams, via the workstream entity once it ships) and collects each session's `artifacts_produced` field.

3. The utility queries git for commits touching the workstream's deliverable directory since the workstream began (deliverable directory is configurable per workstream; for the governance workstream it is `PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/governance-schema-specs/` plus `PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/close-out-payloads/` plus `PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/prompts/` plus `PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/schema-design-kickoff-*.md`).

4. The utility cross-references the two: any commit whose deliverable path is not referenced in any session record's `artifacts_produced` text is reported as a stale-state signal, with the commit hash, date, and path.

5. A one-line addition to `crmbuilder/CLAUDE.md` under "Session orientation protocol" instructing V2-engaged sessions to run the reconciliation check at session start.

6. A one-line addition to the kickoff-prompt template (and to each remaining per-entity schema-design kickoff prompt's Pre-flight section — work_ticket, close_out_payload, deposit_event — and to the kickoff-prompt-generator pattern for future workstreams) instructing pre-flight to invoke the utility against the workstream identifier before the first architectural question.

7. The utility is run once against the current governance-entity-schema-design workstream and reports clean (after SES-050's apply lands; the c1d007e commit's deliverable is now referenced in SES-050's `artifacts_produced`, so no stale-state signal should surface).

**Why this is preventative not a fix to current state.** SES-050 already landed and the SES-050 session record's `artifacts_produced` text includes `governance-schema-specs/reference_book.md` and the v1.1 patch. The current state is consistent; the utility prevents the recurrence of the class of issue, not a current data inconsistency.

**Alternatives considered.** (a) Automatic close-out from the sandbox to Doug's local API — infeasible because the sandbox cannot reach `http://127.0.0.1:8765` on Doug's local machine. (b) V2 desktop app showing "git ahead" warnings — would require changing the V2 app and storing a `last_processed_commit_hash` somewhere; heavier than needed and only surfaces the gap when Doug happens to open the app. (c) Co-commit enforcement (a hook that rejects spec commits without paired apply scripts) — fragile and easy to circumvent, and does not catch the "applied locally in sandbox but never pushed" variant. The reconciliation utility addresses every variant — committed-not-pushed, pushed-not-applied, applied-locally-not-mirrored — because it always derives state from origin and the V2 API together.

**Priority.** Routine — the utility is preventative; no current data drift is blocking work. Discharge timing is build-planning conversation's call within the governance-entity-schema-design workstream, or earlier if Doug authorizes a tooling slice between schema-design conversations.
title: Workstream-state reconciliation utility at kickoff pre-flight to prevent git-vs-database state drift

description: Author PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/methodology-code-change-lifecycle.md defining the seven-stage code change lifecycle in V2: a planning_item motivates a planning conversation; the planning conversation produces decisions and one or more work_ticket records; an execution conversation opens against a work_ticket; the execution conversation produces commits; a close-out payload records resolves edges plus work_ticket consumptions plus commits; the apply transaction lands all of it as data; downstream queries walk the chain. Settle seven deferred design decisions plus the blocks direction question in passing. Deferred decisions are: commit identifier strategy (CM-NNN sequence with server-side auto-assignment versus SHA-as-identifier versus both); granularity of resolves edges (per-commit only via commit_resolves_planning_item, per-conversation only via conversation_resolves_planning_item, or both); commit ingestion trigger (manual command invoked by the apply script, git post-commit hook running the apply script, or background polling against git log); commit record fields (the minimum set: sha, message, author, committed_at, repository, branch, parent_sha; the optional set: files_changed_count, files_changed_paths, signed_by); multi-repo scoping (commits to the crmbuilder repo live in the CRMBUILDER engagement DB, commits to the ClevelandBusinessMentoring repo live in the CBM engagement DB — but Claude Code conversations sometimes touch both repos in a single conversation; the methodology must specify whether the conversation record itself is scoped to one engagement and the cross-repo commits are tracked via cross-engagement references, or whether the conversation forks into per-engagement child records); typed addresses relationship_kind for work_ticket-to-planning_item (define a typed kind for queryability versus continue using generic is_about); work_ticket authoring rule scope (only CLAUDE-CODE-PROMPT-*.md files under PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/prompts/, or all single-use kickoff documents including planning-kickoff files, or some other boundary). The blocks direction question: source blocks target meaning target waits for source, versus the inverse — settled by reading the existing PI-024 PI-025 PI-026 chain in the dogfood database and documenting the convention. Acceptance: methodology document committed; all deferred decisions recorded as decisions in the methodology conversation's close-out; kickoff prompt for the PI-028 commit entity schema conversation authored as the methodology conversation's deliverable.
title: Draft Code Change Lifecycle methodology document and settle deferred design decisions

Team deletion is out of scope per the existing managers' conservative-deletion convention; document this.
title: audit-v1.2 Prompt C — team_manager.py deploy-side CHECK→ACT

description: Author CLAUDE-CODE-PROMPT-audit-v1.2-D-role-manager.md. The most substantive new deploy-side module in the series. Adds: espo_impl/core/role_manager.py implementing CHECK→ACT against EspoCRM's Role entity; api_client.py gains get_roles, create_role, update_role methods; RoleStatus enum and RoleResult dataclass in models.py; the scope_access translation layer mapping YAML scope_access blocks to EspoCRM Role record's data field shape (per-entity-per-action permission matrix with assignment scope); system_permissions translation mapping YAML system_permissions keys to EspoCRM Role record's top-level permission columns. description: Author CLAUDE-CODE-PROMPT-audit-v1.2-E-deploy-ordering.md. Adds: a new pipeline step Security in run_worker._run_full, ordered after Workflows per existing orchestration sequence; the step calls team_manager first (teams have no entity dependencies) then role_manager (roles reference entity names that must exist on the target); within-batch dependency resolution ensures security YAMLs are processed last regardless of filename alphabetical position; updated STEP SUMMARY block to include security step status. Resolves a known interaction: the existing backlog notes that the deploy engine processes files in alphabetical order rather than topological order. This prompt adds a security-specific special-case rule (security files always run last); it does NOT solve the general topological-sort backlog issue. Depends on PI-036 and PI-037. Acceptance: a batch containing entity YAMLs and a security.yaml deploys cleanly regardless of filename order; a security YAML referencing an entity not in the batch and not on the server produces a clear error before any API write.
title: audit-v1.2 Prompt E — Section 12.6 deploy ordering and security pipeline step

description: Author CLAUDE-CODE-PROMPT-audit-v1.2-F-role-clause.md. Adds: condition_expression.py gains a new leaf clause variant for role: predicates (Section 12.5.1); AST extension with RoleClause joining the existing LeafClause, AllNode, AnyNode set; parse_condition recognizes role: keys at the leaf level; validate_condition accepts an optional known_roles set and validates role names against it if provided; render_condition emits role clauses in structured form. evaluate_condition is deliberately NOT extended for roles — role-aware evaluation requires current-user context that the deploy engine does not have; the boundary is correct, validators ensure clauses are well-formed and actual evaluation happens in the target CRM at runtime. Depends on PI-034 (RoleDefinition exists). Acceptance: round-trip of a YAML requiredWhen clause with a role predicate parses and renders correctly; compound clauses mixing role and record-state predicates parse correctly; invalid role names (when known_roles is provided) produce specific errors.

description: Author CLAUDE-CODE-PROMPT-audit-v1.2-G-role-aware-visibility.md. Adds: config_loader.py validates that requiredWhen and visibleWhen clauses with role: predicates reference roles declared in the program batch's roles: block; field_manager.py and layout_manager.py translate role-aware clauses into EspoCRM's dynamic-logic JSON when writing to the target; layout-level forRoles: scoping (Section 12.5.2) is implemented as a layout-set selector at deploy time; validator cross-checks ensure every role referenced in any condition expression exists in the batch's roles: block or on the target instance. Acceptance: a YAML with a field requiredWhen clause referencing the Mentor role deploys correctly with role-aware dynamic logic on the target; a layout with forRoles: [Mentor Administrator] deploys only to that role's effective layout; a field with a role clause referencing an undeclared role produces a clear pre-deploy error.

description: Author CLAUDE-CODE-PROMPT-audit-v1.2-I-filtered-tab-audit.md. description: Implement the four decisions from SES-064 (DEC-201 local-canonical with tunneled MCP; DEC-202 MCP-only exposure; DEC-203 marker-driven single deployment; DEC-204 Cloudflare Tunnel on mcp.crmbuilder.com with Access + shared-secret auth). Scope: (a) Code changes delivered as CLAUDE-CODE-PROMPT-*.md files: add --transport {stdio,streamable-http} flag to the crmbuilder-v2-mcp CLI entry point in crmbuilder-v2/src/crmbuilder_v2/cli.py, parameterize the transport in mcp_server/server.py main(), bind the FastMCP HTTP transport to 127.0.0.1 with a configurable port, add a shared-secret header middleware to the HTTP transport that 401s missing or wrong secrets, address the engagement-marker-mid-process risk identified in DEC-203 by either re-reading current_engagement.json per request or failing loud on marker change with restart-required signal. (b) Operational steps: move crmbuilder.com nameservers from current registrar to Cloudflare (free plan); install cloudflared on Doug's laptop and configure as a launchd service; create the named tunnel and DNS record routing mcp.crmbuilder.com to 127.0.0.1:<mcp_port>; create the Cloudflare Access policy requiring Google login (Doug's identity) for the mcp.crmbuilder.com hostname; register the MCP URL in claude.ai's MCP integration with the shared-secret custom header. (c) Verification: smoke-test all 44 MCP tools end-to-end from a claude.ai conversation against both CRMBUILDER and CBM engagements (flip the engagement marker between runs); confirm an unauthenticated request from a non-Doug browser is blocked at the Access layer; confirm a request with wrong shared secret is blocked at the MCP layer; confirm engagement marker drives the right database. (d) Documentation: README section in crmbuilder-v2/ covering remote deployment setup; entry in repo CLAUDE.md under the v2 section describing the remote MCP URL and auth model; the deployment kickoff prompt itself (the in-flight follow-on conversation deliverable, drafted before any of the above starts).
title: V2 remote-access deployment: expose MCP server publicly so claude.ai can read and write the v2 governance database

description:
vocab._kinds_for_pair at lines 354-360 admits target_type="reference" as a valid target for source_type="deposit_event" with kind="deposit_event_wrote_record". vocab.ENTITY_TYPES at lines 250-289 does NOT include "reference" — so RELATIONSHIP_RULES (the dict computed at line 377-381 as the cartesian product of ENTITY_TYPES × ENTITY_TYPES with valid kinds) never generates the ("deposit_event", "reference") key. The API's POST validator reads RELATIONSHIP_RULES; POSTs with target_type="reference" fail the dict lookup and return HTTP 422. The two specs (close_out_payload.md and deposit_event.md) describe wrote_record as covering "sessions, decisions, planning items, and references" — the spec aligns with _kinds_for_pair's admission but is contradicted by ENTITY_TYPES.

Discovered at PI-026's apply time (per DEC-215's context). Resolution options: (A) Add "reference" to vocab.ENTITY_TYPES. Cascades: extend the CHECK constraints ck_ref_source_type and ck_ref_target_type in an Alembic migration (admit "reference" as a valid type); the v0.3 cascading ReferenceCreateDialog inherits the change automatically via RELATIONSHIP_RULES regeneration. Consequence: future real-time close-out applies will carry wrote_record edges to reference targets; PI-026's historical DEPs remain reference-orphan (immutable per born-terminal lifecycle).
(B) Remove the "reference" admission from vocab._kinds_for_pair at lines 354-360. Update close_out_payload.md and deposit_event.md to drop "references" from the wrote_record target-type list. Consequence: the apply path symmetrically skips references for all backfilled and real-time close-outs going forward; aligns with Phase 1 and PI-026's actual pattern.
(C) Some hybrid (e.g., admit reference-target wrote_record only under specific status transitions, or carry the reference-target info in records_summary.references count without an explicit edge).

Resolution requires deciding whether reference-as-wrote_record-target adds enough audit value to justify the schema-extension cost. Phase 1 said no; PI-026's DEC-206 said yes but couldn't implement; DEC-215 says no for now. PI-023's reconciliation utility may surface additional context once it can analyze the full apply-event graph.
title: Resolve vocab.py schema-vs-spec contradiction for reference targets in deposit_event_wrote_record edges

description:
ses_030.json and ses_036.json on disk in PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/close-out-payloads/ share the same session title ("Styling Conversation 2 — build planning: ui-PRD-v0.6..."); both reference decisions DEC-105, DEC-106, and DEC-107 in their decisions arrays. The database has both SES-030 and SES-036 as separate session records. The decided_in edges in references.json point from DEC-105/106/107 to SES-036, not SES-030. ses_030.json also claims a session-is_about-planning_item edge to PI-001 that doesn't exist in the snapshot for SES-030.

Surfaced as DEC-208's case (c) — "four payload references in ses_030.json don't resolve in either direction" — and as a future cleanup candidate in SES-066's in_flight_at_end. PI-026's backfill resolver flagged the 4 references as unresolvable (per DEC-208's forward-then-reverse-then-skip strategy) and skipped them; DEP-028 (the DEP for ses_030's apply) carries records_summary.references=0. PI-026's COP for ses_030 (COP-017) is in applied status and carries a producing_conversation edge to CONV-027 — so the records ARE attributed to SES-030, even though the decided_in edges in the snapshot point to SES-036.

Resolution requires reading the conversations table, the original close-out file commit timestamps, and the decision-creation timestamps to determine which session is the canonical apply. Hypotheses: (a) SES-030 was an aborted/re-tried apply attempt; SES-036 is the successful re-apply; both records should be preserved historically but one should supersede the other. (b) The two sessions are genuinely separate apply events that happened to share a title; the decided_in edges should be back-filled for SES-030 (SES-036's decided_in edges may be misattributed). (c) Some other narrative.

Resolution options once canonicality is determined: (A) Merge SES-030 into SES-036 — redirect any inbound references to SES-036 + delete SES-030 (soft-delete via the session deleted_at column). (B) Mark one as supersedes the other via a session → session supersedes edge (same-type supersession is admitted by vocab._kinds_for_pair). (C) Back-fill the 4 missing decided_in references for SES-030 if SES-030 was a real apply event with its own writes; DEP-028's records_summary.references would then need amendment — but DEP-028 is born-terminal and immutable, so this option requires accepting the records_summary undercount.
title: Resolve ses_030 / ses_036 duplicate-session artifact and the 4 unresolvable references ses_030's payload claims

description: Surfaced during PI-023's planning conversation while testing reconcile.py against the current db-export snapshot. ses_056.json's references array contains two entries with relationship="blocks": (planning_item PI-025 → planning_item PI-024) and (planning_item PI-026 → planning_item PI-025). The v0.8 methodology rename dropped "blocks" from the relationship vocabulary in favor of the directionally-renamed "blocked_by" (with source/target semantics inverted). The corresponding edges in the references snapshot exist as: (planning_item PI-025 blocked_by PI-024) and (planning_item PI-026 blocked_by PI-025). The references are not missing — they were renamed at vocabulary level. The ses_056.json file on disk was authored before the rename and has not been retroactively migrated. PI-023's reconcile.py classifies the payload's two references as Class 2 (record-claims-vs-record-presence) findings because the (source_type, source_id, target_type, target_id, relationship) 5-tuple lookup against the references snapshot fails — the snapshot stores relationship_kind="blocked_by" with source/target swapped per the directional rename. PI-023's allowlist carries `pi-048-ses-056-stale-blocks-vocabulary` citing PI-048 as the canonical record. Two resolution paths: (i) author a one-off script that walks all close-out payload JSON files in PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/close-out-payloads/, finds entries with relationship="blocks" or any other v0.8-renamed relationship kind, rewrites them to the renamed form, and commits the migrated files. The allowlist entry can then be removed from reconciliation-allowlist.yaml. (ii) Author a governance decision formally accepting stale-vocab in historical close-out payload files as an immutable archival convention — historical payloads represent the system's state at their authoring moment; rewriting them later loses fidelity to that moment. In this case the allowlist entry stays, and additional historical payloads with similar patterns get added to its scope (or to similar entries for other renamed kinds). The choice between (i) and (ii) hinges on whether close-out payload files are treated as immutable historical records or as continuously-migrated current-state representations. The vocab.py comment at the `blocks → blocked_by` rename site implies the rename is a one-way migration (the previous kind is 'dropped from the vocabulary'); applied strictly, this argues for (i). PI-048's resolution is deferred to a future workstream alongside PI-046 (vocab schema-vs-spec contradiction) and PI-047 (ses_030/ses_036 duplicate-session); all three are governance-data-quality cleanup planning items eligible for inclusion in a single future workstream lane.
title: Migrate stale 'blocks' relationship references in ses_056.json (and any other historical close-out payloads with the same pattern) to the v0.8-renamed 'blocked_by' relationship kind — or formally accept stale-vocab in historical payloads as an immutable archival convention

description:
Scope per DEC-226. Four phases.

PHASE 1 — Path B feasibility (timebox 60 minutes). Investigate whether Cloudflare Access can be configured as an OIDC IdP that claude.ai's connector accepts: (a) read Cloudflare's SaaS Application docs and OIDC documentation; (b) verify whether Cloudflare exposes MCP-spec-compliant Protected Resource Metadata (RFC 9728) or whether we'd need to serve our own pointing at Cloudflare's authorization endpoints; (c) identify the issuer/audience values claude.ai expects vs. what Cloudflare emits; (d) check whether Cloudflare's OAuth flow supports PKCE per the MCP 2.1 spec. If Path B is feasible within reasonable Cloudflare-config work, commit to Path B and SKIP Phase 2's implementation. If Path B is not feasible or fails the timebox, proceed to Phase 2.

PHASE 2 — Path A implementation (only if Path B doesn't pan out). Implement OAuth 2.1 + PKCE on the v2 MCP server: (1) Protected Resource Metadata at /.well-known/oauth-protected-resource per RFC 9728 (authorization server URI, supported scopes, bearer token methods); (2) Authorization Server Metadata at /.well-known/oauth-authorization-server with /authorize and /token endpoints; (3) /authorize endpoint with minimal consent UI (single-user system; consent UI can be terse — 'Allow claude.ai to access CRMBuilder v2 governance database? Yes/No'); (4) /token endpoint with code-for-token exchange and PKCE verification; (5) bearer-token validation middleware on MCP routes (replaces SharedSecretMiddleware from SES-072 slice B); (6) token storage in v2.db (new tables: oauth_clients, oauth_authorization_codes, oauth_access_tokens — schema designed in the conversation); (7) optionally /register for Dynamic Client Registration (defer if Anthropic-held credentials registration mode works fine — claude.ai's Advanced Settings already accepts pre-registered Client ID/Secret).

PHASE 3 — Cleanup (both paths). Remove the SharedSecretMiddleware from slice B; remove mcp-secret persistence (file at ~/.config/crmbuilder/mcp-secret, env var CRMBUILDER_V2_MCP_SHARED_SECRET, the startup hard-fail check); update docs to reflect the new auth model; remove X-CRMBuilder-Secret guidance from the PI-045 step-5 kickoff and any other kickoff files; rotate-and-archive the Service Token credentials (keep in place but document as defense-in-depth, not the primary auth path); update CLAUDE.md's PI-045/auth-model sections.

PHASE 4 — claude.ai connector registration (the PI-045 step-5 work that this PI unblocks). Register against the new OAuth-enabled mcp.crmbuilder.ai endpoint; verify all 44 MCP tools discoverable; run the smoke test per the original PI-045 step-5 kickoff (read surface + write surface; engagement marker drives routing; three auth-path negative cases); ship the documentation deliverables per PI-045 part (d).

OUT OF SCOPE: replacing the Cloudflare Tunnel or Cloudflare Access edge (both stay); expanding to multi-user (single-user system; consent UI is minimal); persistent token storage with refresh tokens (a single long-lived bearer per claude.ai connection is sufficient — re-auth on expiry is acceptable for a single-user system).

On PI-049 completion, PI-045 step 5 + smoke test + documentation can execute in a follow-up conversation (or be folded into PI-049's Phase 4).
title: v2 MCP server OAuth 2.1 + PKCE implementation: enable claude.ai custom connector registration by replacing the X-CRMBuilder-Secret shared-secret middleware with OAuth-token-based auth (with a timeboxed Path B feasibility check before committing to Path A)

description:
Slice C's enumerate_commits.py uses a since..HEAD range model that assumes a single active workstream per repository. The SES-074 build closure (PI-030's first end-to-end use) discovered this assumption breaks when multiple workstreams interleave commits on the main branch: between PI-030 slice A's parent and the closure's HEAD, the crmbuilder main-branch log contained 8 commits NOT owned by PI-030 (audit-v1.2 Prompts G/H/I, PI-045 code-changes apply, PI-045 step-5 operational kickoff). The kickoff's default mitigation (--since-sha flag) doesn't help; parallel commits sit within any since..HEAD range. SES-074 worked around the limitation by hand-curating its three slice SHAs into the payload's commits section per DEC-233.

Scope: extend enumerate_commits.py with an explicit-list mode (suggested flag: --commits SHA1,SHA2,...) that emits commit-record JSON for exactly the named SHAs from the working tree, in chronological order. Bypasses the since..HEAD range calculation entirely. CLI surface analogous to the existing single-repo and multi-repo invocation patterns. Tests cover: (a) flag-and-positional mutual exclusion with --since-sha; (b) explicit-list emission order matches git's chronological order regardless of input order; (c) unknown SHA raises a clear error with the offending SHA named; (d) the explicit-list mode honors the same --branch and --repo-root-override flags as the range mode. Methodology §5.5 (already amended in SES-074 to clarify the parallel-workstream case) may gain a sentence pointing at the explicit-list flag once it ships.

This planning item may be subsumed by PI-033's Phase 4 historical-commits backfill — which confronts the same parallel-workstream problem at much larger scale across both repos — if PI-033 ends up needing the same explicit-list flag and ships it first. Status stays Open until either is built; whichever ships the flag resolves this PI.
title: Extend enumerate_commits.py with explicit-list mode for parallel-workstream closures

description:
Implement deploy support for Section 12.5 (Role-Aware Visibility) and Section 12.7 (Field-Level Permissions) on EspoCRM 9.x. v1.2 left both sections NOT_SUPPORTED per DEC-243 (12.5) and prior deferrals (12.7). The right implementation path requires real wire-format research; current best candidates are documented in DEC-243 and the schema §12.5 Deploy Support sub-section. 

Scope items to investigate at PI-051 kickoff: (1) Dynamic Handler JavaScript generation — `client/custom/src/views/<entity>/record/detail.js` generation per entity with role-aware visibility logic; requires file-system writes (no REST API path) and an audit-side reverse-engineering strategy for round-trip; or accept that this surface is NOT_AUDITABLE and stays write-only. (2) Teams-as-proxies-for-Roles translation — auto-generate a Team per Role at deploy; bind users to teams as a separate operator step; use EspoCRM 8.1's current-user-teams Dynamic Logic condition. Requires conflating Team and Role semantics or accepting that operators see auto-generated teams in the EspoCRM admin UI. (3) Upstream EspoCRM feature request — file a Dynamic Logic role-condition feature request and wait. Not actionable as a planning item but worth tracking. (4) Layout Sets + Teams (forRoles variant deploy path) — separate from 12.5.1 deploy; layout-level role scoping can use Layout Sets bound to auto-generated mirror-Teams even if leaf-clause role conditions remain NOT_SUPPORTED.

For §12.7 (field-level permissions): out of scope for v1.2 because the schema sub-section was deferred from v1.1. v1.4 should consider §12.5 and §12.7 holistically since both are role-keyed permission surfaces with similar deploy mechanics on EspoCRM (probably some combination of role-record fieldOverridesPermission columns and dynamic-logic for visibility).

Predecessors: audit-v1.2 series complete (this close-out). Schema spec §12.5 and §12.6 patched at v1.2; §12.7 sub-section still in draft state from prior work. Successor: actual v1.4 schema-spec revisions plus implementation prompts. No active commitment to a target date — PI-051 enters the backlog at status Open.
title: audit-v1.4 — Section 12.5 role-aware visibility deploy implementation alongside Section 12.7 field-level permissions

description: Per DEC-245's architectural pivot away from MCP-via-claude.ai (blocked by upstream Anthropic bug per DEC-244). Scope: build a chat UI that gives Doug Claude conversations with access to the v2 governance database tool surface. Architecture: Python client using the Anthropic SDK with native tools=[...] definitions, calling the REST API at 127.0.0.1:8765 directly. Reuse `crmbuilder_v2.mcp_server.tools` module functions (already wrap httpx → REST API) as the native tool definitions — no rewrite required, their signatures and docstrings can be auto-converted to Anthropic API tool schemas. Loop: read user input → messages.create(tools=...) → execute any tool_use blocks via httpx → feed tool_result back → repeat until Claude responds without further tool calls. Phase 1 (proof of concept, ~30 min): a terminal-based ~50 LOC Python script that demonstrates the loop end-to-end with a handful of tools. Phase 2 (production surface): pick one of (a) terminal-based UI with Rich/Textual, (b) PySide6 chat tab inside the existing CRMBuilder desktop app, (c) Streamlit/Gradio web prototype, (d) custom FastAPI + React/HTMX web app on the existing Cloudflare Tunnel — choice deferred until Phase 1 completes. No MCP HTTP transport involved. MCP server in stdio mode for Claude Desktop continues to work unaffected. Cloudflare Tunnel + Access + Managed OAuth infrastructure preserved on disk (Path B work in commit fc4690c) for future use if/when Anthropic fixes the connector bug.
title: Chat UI on Anthropic API with native tool definitions — pilot and build
